sections.heading: PCV coverage improved nationally, but the third dose still trails the first
sections.body: Among children aged 12 to 23 months, national PCV 1 coverage rose from 81.8 percent in 2022 to 90.9 percent in 2025. PCV 2 increased from 78.0 to 85.1 percent, and PCV 3 increased from 71.5 to 77.7 percent. The sequence still shows a 13.2-point drop from PCV 1 to PCV 3 in 2025, so the main national gap is not first-dose reach alone but continuation through the third dose.

sections.heading: Top regional coverage stayed high through the third PCV dose
sections.body: Ilocos Region had the highest 2025 PCV 3 coverage at 97.1 percent, after PCV 1 coverage of 100.0 percent and PCV 2 coverage of 99.6 percent. Western Visayas and Davao Region also remained above 90 percent on PCV 3, at 92.1 and 91.3 percent. These regions show that high first-dose reach can be paired with relatively strong completion of the PCV sequence.

sections.heading: BARMM remained the lowest regional outlier across the full PCV sequence
sections.body: BARMM had the lowest 2025 coverage for all three PCV doses: 52.6 percent for PCV 1, 30.9 percent for PCV 2, and 21.2 percent for PCV 3. Its first-to-third-dose drop was 31.4 points, the largest among regions. The data show both weak initial reach and substantial loss across the sequence.

sections.heading: Some regions with high first-dose coverage had large completion drop-offs
sections.body: Central Visayas reached 91.8 percent for PCV 1 in 2025 but fell to 68.2 percent for PCV 3, a 23.6-point drop. The Negros Island Region reached 100.0 percent for PCV 1 and 95.0 percent for PCV 2, but PCV 3 was 79.8 percent, a 20.2-point drop from the first dose. The inference is that missed later doses, not just first contact, shape regional PCV gaps.

sections.heading: Several regions improved sharply from 2022 to 2025
sections.body: Davao Region's PCV 3 coverage rose from 61.9 percent in 2022 to 91.3 percent in 2025, and SOCCSKSARGEN rose from 59.9 to 84.7 percent. MIMAROPA Region also increased from 60.2 to 78.2 percent. These gains show that regional ordering changed in places where later-dose coverage improved faster than the national average.

sections.heading: Girls had slightly higher third-dose PCV coverage than boys
sections.body: PCV 1 coverage was nearly the same for male and female children in 2025, at 91.0 and 90.6 percent. The gap widened by PCV 3: female children reached 80.1 percent, while male children reached 75.7 percent. The sex difference is modest compared with regional and card-status gaps, but it appears more clearly at the end of the sequence.

sections.heading: Higher birth order was linked to lower PCV completion
sections.body: First-born children had 93.8 percent PCV 1 coverage and 83.0 percent PCV 3 coverage in 2025. Children of birth order 6 or higher had lower coverage at every dose, with 85.8 percent for PCV 1 and 64.8 percent for PCV 3. Their first-to-third-dose drop was 21.0 points, nearly double the 10.8-point drop among first-born children.

sections.heading: Vaccination card status showed the widest non-regional divide
sections.body: Children whose vaccination card was seen had 97.6 percent PCV 1 coverage and 91.7 percent PCV 3 coverage in 2025. Among children who never had a card, the corresponding levels were 40.0 and 25.0 percent. Children whose card was not seen or who no longer had a card were between those groups for PCV 1 at 83.3 percent, but fell to 50.3 percent by PCV 3. This does not prove the card causes vaccination, but documented contact is strongly aligned with completing the PCV sequence.

sections.heading: Urban and rural PCV coverage was very similar in 2025
sections.body: Urban children had 91.6 percent PCV 1 coverage, 85.4 percent PCV 2 coverage, and 77.9 percent PCV 3 coverage in 2025. Rural children were close behind at 90.2, 84.8, and 77.5 percent. The residence gap was therefore small compared with the differences by region, birth order, card status, education, and wealth.

sections.heading: Mother's education showed a clear gradient in PCV completion
sections.body: Among children whose mothers had Grades 1 to 6 education, 2025 PCV 3 coverage was 64.9 percent, after PCV 1 coverage of 82.8 percent. Among children whose mothers had college education, PCV 3 coverage was 81.5 percent, after PCV 1 coverage of 92.6 percent. Post-secondary values were higher, at 95.5 percent for PCV 3, but the display data flagged them, while the no-education group was suppressed in 2025. Within the usable rows, higher maternal schooling is associated with stronger completion.

sections.heading: Wealth differences persisted across all three PCV doses
sections.body: Children in the lowest wealth quintile had 84.5 percent PCV 1 coverage and 68.5 percent PCV 3 coverage in 2025. Children in the highest quintile had 96.8 percent PCV 1 coverage and 87.0 percent PCV 3 coverage. The gap was 12.3 points for the first dose and 18.5 points for the third dose, indicating that wealth differences are larger at completion than at initial PCV reach.

takeaways: National PCV coverage improved from 2022 to 2025, but PCV 3 coverage still lagged PCV 1 by 13.2 percentage points.
Regional variation was large, with PCV 3 coverage ranging from 97.1 percent in Ilocos Region to 21.2 percent in BARMM.
Children with a seen vaccination card, lower birth order, higher maternal education, and higher wealth were more likely to complete the PCV sequence.
